title: Art Work

jokes.text: He's fighting you?
jokes.setup: Tony asks how they know they can't lose on a fight bet
jokes.punchline: Suggesting the only sure bet is if Tony himself is fighting
jokes.explanation: Quick, economical putdown of Tony's fighting ability delivered with perfect timing

dialogue.speaker: Unknown Character
dialogue.line: How do you know you can't lose on a fight bet?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: The only sure bet is if I'm fighting.

jokes.text: Uh... the horse racing. No. Uh... football. Nope. Badminton?
jokes.setup: Latka is asked what he's betting on but doesn't know
jokes.punchline: His guesses get progressively more absurd, ending with badminton
jokes.explanation: Classic Latka confusion escalating to absurdity - badminton is a perfect final guess

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: I'm betting on the horses?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: The dogs?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: The cock fighting?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: The badminton?

jokes.text: Elaine, Elaine... this fellow has a broken hand, and I could win many dollars, and that would make my family and me very happy.
jokes.setup: Alex is asked what he would do in Latka's position
jokes.punchline: He responds by perfectly mimicking Latka's speech patterns and innocent worldview
jokes.explanation: Alex's perfect imitation of Latka's speech and innocent logic is both affectionate and funny

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, if I were Latka, I'd probably say something like: 'Oh, is very simple! In my country, we have saying: when life gives you problem, you make problem go away with big stick!' And then I'd look confused about why everyone is laughing.

jokes.text: You may laugh, but I know people who talk like that.
jokes.setup: Alex has just done a perfect impression of Latka
jokes.punchline: He deadpans that he knows people who actually talk that way
jokes.explanation: Meta joke acknowledging the absurdity of Latka's character while keeping it grounded

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I know people who actually talk that way.

jokes.text: I have one very firm rule in my life: I don't eat at the same table, bet on the same side, or climb on the same airplane as losers.
jokes.setup: Louie is asked if he wants to bet too
jokes.punchline: His elaborate rule about avoiding losers in increasingly specific scenarios
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie - elaborate, mean-spirited rule delivered with complete conviction. The escalating specificity is expertly crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: No, no, no. I got a rule. I never bet with a guy who's got a losing record. And I never bet with a guy who knows a guy with a losing record. And I never bet with a guy who's ever been in the same room as a guy who knows a guy with a losing record.

jokes.text: Be gone, Iggy.
jokes.setup: Louie has just insulted the group
jokes.punchline: He dismisses them with a grandiose command using 'Iggy' as a dismissive term
jokes.explanation: Typical Louie pomposity with a creative insult name

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Iggy, get these bums outta here!

jokes.text: Yeah, well, the guy Johnson's fighting is named Johnson, too.
jokes.setup: Tony confirms their fighter Johnson is going to win
jokes.punchline: Alex reveals both fighters are named Johnson, invalidating their 'sure thing'
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup and payoff revealing the flaw in their 'sure thing' bet. Classic sitcom misdirection.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Johnson's gonna win, I'm telling you. It's a sure thing.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Tony, both fighters are named Johnson.

jokes.text: We got a 50-50 chance on a sure thing.
jokes.setup: Tony realizes they have two fighters with the same name
jokes.punchline: His contradictory logic about having 50-50 odds on a 'sure thing'
jokes.explanation: Perfect oxymoron delivered with Tony's characteristic confidence in flawed logic

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: We got two fighters with the same name. So no matter who wins, we win!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: That doesn't make any sense.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Sure it does. If one of them wins, we got a winner named that. If the other one wins, we still got a winner with that name. It's a sure thing!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Tony, those are fifty-fifty odds on any fight.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Yeah, but this is a sure thing!

jokes.text: You must all promise to keep me informed of your flight plans.
jokes.setup: The group has just made a disastrous bet
jokes.punchline: Louie wants to track their travel so he can avoid being on the same plane
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to his earlier 'rule' with darkly funny implications about their luck

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Alright, listen up. I'm gonna need to know every flight you people are taking. Every single one.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'm gonna have my secretary mark 'em all down, and I'm gonna make damn sure I'm on a different plane. Different airline if I can help it.

jokes.text: if you value our friendship, you will not put that smirk into words.
jokes.setup: Elaine is clearly smirking at their betting disaster
jokes.punchline: Alex preemptively begs her not to verbalize her obvious 'I told you so'
jokes.explanation: Clever way to acknowledge Elaine's vindication without letting her say it

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Don't. Don't say it.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I wasn't going to say anything.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I can see it written all over your face.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Say what?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: 'I told you so.' I know. You told me so. You were right, I was wrong. Just... don't say it out loud.

jokes.text: Every day you come in here with some sure thing and it never works out.
jokes.setup: The guys have just lost their 'sure thing' bet
jokes.punchline: Elaine points out their pattern of failed 'sure things'
jokes.explanation: Straightforward observation that sets up the contrast with rich people's success

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You know, I've been watching you guys, and I've noticed something. Every time you say something's a 'sure thing,' it's not.

jokes.text: You bet on painters?
jokes.setup: Elaine suggests investing in art
jokes.punchline: Tony misunderstands and thinks they'd bet on painters like racehorses
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tony misunderstanding - applies his gambling mindset to art in a literal way

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Art? You want to invest in art? That's a good idea. You know, I had a cousin once, he invested in a painter.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: The guy's name was Giuseppe. Every week, my cousin would go down to the track, you know, and he'd bet on Giuseppe.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Giuseppe was a beautiful painter. Fast. Real fast. The guy could paint a whole room in like an hour and a half.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: But then one day, Giuseppe, he gets injured. Fell off a ladder. Never painted again. My cousin lost a fortune.

jokes.text: I don't think you ever had any inside information. I think you were just trying to impress us.
jokes.setup: Elaine keeps refusing to help with art investment
jokes.punchline: Alex uses reverse psychology by questioning her credibility
jokes.explanation: Classic reverse psychology that immediately works on Elaine

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You know, Elaine, I'm starting to think you don't actually know anything about art.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What? I know plenty about art!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Really? Because a real art expert would jump at the chance to help someone invest. The fact that you keep refusing makes me think you're not as knowledgeable as you claim.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Fine! I'll help you!

jokes.text: don't deny us a Duffin.
jokes.setup: The group is pressuring Elaine about artist Max Duffin
jokes.punchline: Bobby makes a pun on 'Duffin' and 'nothing' while pleading
jokes.explanation: Mild pun that works in context of their pleading but isn't particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Come on, Elaine, give the guy a chance. What's the worst that could happen? You go out with him, you have a terrible time, and you never see him again. It's not like you're duffin' to lose!

jokes.text: Where the hell did I learn that?
jokes.setup: Jim has just perfectly recited a lengthy Kipling poem
jokes.punchline: He's genuinely puzzled about how he knows it
jokes.explanation: Perfect capstone to the poem moment - Jim's confusion makes it even funnier and more endearing

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: How do I know that? I don't even like poetry.

jokes.text: after the artist... dies.
jokes.setup: Elaine is explaining why Max Duffin's art will appreciate
jokes.punchline: Her uncomfortable pause before saying 'dies' emphasizes the morbid nature
jokes.explanation: The pause and discomfort set up the ghoulish nature of their investment strategy

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well, his paintings will be worth a lot more when he... dies.

jokes.text: Can he eat solid food? Does he recognize visitors? Is he on any machines? I mean, is there a plug somebody might accidentally... Whoop! Trip over?
jokes.setup: Louie is asking about the dying artist's condition
jokes.punchline: His questions get increasingly morbid, ending with suggesting they could 'accidentally' kill him
jokes.explanation: Peak Louie darkness - the escalation is perfectly structured and the 'whoop!' is a brilliant touch

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: So how's he doing? The artist guy?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Is he gonna make it?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: How much longer we talking here?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know, if he's really suffering, we could always make it look like an accident.

jokes.text: buying one of the old geezer's paintings is like betting he's going to die. We can't lose.
jokes.setup: Louie explains his interest in the investment
jokes.punchline: He frames art investment as literally betting on death with confidence they can't lose
jokes.explanation: Louie's brutal honesty about the morbid nature of their plan, delivered with his characteristic confidence

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know what I'm investing in? Art. Fine art. It's a sure thing.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: See, you buy a painting from some artist, and you just wait. Wait for what? For the artist to die. And when they die, the painting's worth ten times what you paid for it.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: It's foolproof. You can't lose. Either the artist dies and you make a fortune, or they keep living and you break even. Either way, we win.

jokes.text: All those in favor of keeping Louie out of this deal signify so by spitting on his shoes.
jokes.setup: They're trying to vote on whether to include Louie
jokes.punchline: Bobby suggests an absurd voting method of spitting on Louie's shoes
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully absurd alternative to normal voting that shows their feelings about Louie

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I got an idea. Let's vote on it. All in favor of including Louie, spit on his shoes.

jokes.text: I'll just sit over here turn my back away and, uh, you know, you just pretend I'm not in the room, and I'll respect the confidentiality of your discussion.
jokes.setup: Louie is asked to give them privacy to discuss including him
jokes.punchline: His elaborate promise to respect confidentiality while obviously planning to eavesdrop
jokes.explanation: Louie's transparent attempt to seem trustworthy while clearly planning to spy

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Privacy? Of course, you got it. I'm gonna go in my office, close the door, put on my headphones, turn up the radio real loud. You won't hear a peep outta me. I guarantee it.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Of course, I'll have the door open just a crack so I can hear if anybody needs me. And I'll position myself right here by the filing cabinet where I can see through the crack. And maybe I'll use this little mirror to get a better angle.

jokes.text: I hate him.
jokes.setup: Louie has just made his obviously disingenuous promise about privacy
jokes.punchline: Tony's flat, immediate response expressing everyone's feelings
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and timing - says exactly what everyone is thinking

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Privacy? Louie, you wouldn't know privacy if it moved in with you and paid half the rent.

jokes.text: 350 Brokniks. How much is that in American money? Nothing.
jokes.setup: Latka states his contribution in his native currency
jokes.punchline: It translates to nothing in American money
jokes.explanation: Perfect Latka moment - sets up expectation with official-sounding currency, deflated by reality

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: I contribute 50,000 drachmas.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: That translates to nothing in American money.

jokes.text: Hey, I can buy and sell Latka.
jokes.setup: Jim contributes only $18 while Latka has nothing
jokes.punchline: Jim boasts he can 'buy and sell' Latka despite having almost nothing himself
jokes.explanation: Jim's delusional pride about his minimal financial advantage over Latka

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I can buy and sell you, Latka.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: With what? You only have eighteen dollars!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Well, I could buy you for ten and sell you for fifteen.

jokes.text: Raise it or lose it, Wheeler.
jokes.setup: They're voting on whether to include Louie and Jim is hesitating
jokes.punchline: Louie threatens Jim to vote for him
jokes.explanation: Typical Louie intimidation with a snappy phrase

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You better vote for me, Jim.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Or what?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Or I'll tell everyone about that time you cried at the movie.

jokes.text: I've heard that there's a thrill associated with coming in contact with great art, but I never realized how true it was until this very moment.
jokes.setup: Bobby is at the art gallery looking at prints
jokes.punchline: He thinks he's having a profound art experience but he's looking at worthless prints
jokes.explanation: Bobby's pretentious art appreciation undercut by the fact he's looking at prints

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: This is incredible. I've never felt anything like this before. The colors, the composition... it's speaking to my soul.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: This artist really understands the human condition. This piece is worth thousands, maybe tens of thousands.

dialogue.speaker: Gallery Employee
dialogue.line: Actually, those are fifty-cent prints from a clearance bin.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Oh.

jokes.text: Bobby, these are just prints. They're copies of original paintings. They're almost worthless. Oh.
jokes.setup: Bobby is amazed by the 'great art' he's experiencing
jokes.punchline: His deflation when told they're just cheap copies
jokes.explanation: Simple deflation joke about mistaking reproductions for originals

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: This is amazing! I'm looking at real masterpieces!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well, not exactly. They're copies.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Copies? You mean these aren't originals?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: No, they're cheap reproductions from a department store.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Oh.

jokes.text: Oh, good, you gave Latka the day off. Yeah, I told him that all he had to do is fix the problem in cab 643, he could come with us. Oh, did you get it fixed? Uh... almost. What's that? Uh... uh... I don't know. That's the problem.
jokes.setup: Elaine notices Latka isn't there, Alex explains the condition for him coming
jokes.punchline: Alex reveals Latka brought an unidentified car part with him, showing he didn't fix anything
jokes.explanation: Visual gag about Latka's mechanical 'skills' - he removed something but doesn't know what it is

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Where's Latka?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: He said he wouldn't come back until he fixed that car.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well, he's here.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: He fixed it?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: I fixed it.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: What's that?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: I don't know.

jokes.text: What's that? Uh... uh... I don't know. That's the problem.
jokes.setup: Elaine notices something wrong with cab 643
jokes.punchline: Latka doesn't know what the mysterious part is, which IS the problem
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag setup with Latka's mechanical incompetence as the punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: There's something wrong with cab 643. What is this part?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: I don't know.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well, that's the problem!

jokes.text: As luck would have it, we've got the same tie size.
jokes.setup: Jim was supposed to dress up but only wore a borrowed tie
jokes.punchline: He claims they have the same 'tie size' as if ties come in specific sizes like shoes
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd Jim logic - treating ties like they have specific sizes

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: We're the same tie size.

jokes.text: This is worth 2,000 bucks? ... For 2,000 bucks, you'd think they'd throw in a name.
jokes.setup: Louie sees their painting is untitled
jokes.punchline: He thinks they should include a title for the price like it's a service add-on
jokes.explanation: Louie's crude consumer mindset applied to fine art

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What's the title of this painting?

dialogue.speaker: Artist
dialogue.line: It doesn't have one.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: No title? How much is it?

dialogue.speaker: Artist
dialogue.line: Five hundred dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Five hundred? For a painting with no title? That's like buying a car with no name. You gotta charge extra for a title — that's a service!

jokes.text: But there's only one seat left in the front. Not for long.
jokes.setup: Tony wants to sit in front but there's only one seat
jokes.punchline: Louie implies he'll forcibly remove someone, then presumably does
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie solution to a problem - intimidation and force

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: There's only one seat left up front.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Then somebody's gonna have to give it up.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Who?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Whoever's in it.

jokes.text: Way to go, Louie.
jokes.setup: Louie has apparently forcibly acquired a front seat
jokes.punchline: Tony's approval of Louie's crude but effective methods
jokes.explanation: Simple approval of Louie's actions, gets a mild laugh

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Hey, Louie, how'd you get the front seat?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I told the guy if he didn't move, I'd break his legs.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: That's beautiful, Louie. That's real beautiful.

jokes.text: Most of my assets are tied up in investments... right now all my money's in a nursing home in Jersey.
jokes.setup: Louie claims his assets are tied up in investments
jokes.punchline: He owns a nursing home, which sounds legitimate until he explains further
jokes.explanation: Sets up what seems like a legitimate investment before revealing Louie's involvement

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I own a nursing home.

dialogue.speaker: Other Character
dialogue.line: That sounds like a legitimate investment.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I own the building. The patients are paying me to let them stay there, but I don't actually provide any care or services. I just collect the money and let them fend for themselves.

jokes.text: Those people don't eat three meals a day. I mean, their memories are so bad they don't know whether they had three meals or a cracker. I say let them sleep.
jokes.setup: Louie explains his nursing home investment problems
jokes.punchline: He suggests exploiting elderly people's memory problems to save money on food
jokes.explanation: Peak Louie evil - exploiting vulnerable elderly people with shocking casual delivery

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know what my problem is? These old people, they eat too much. So I figure, I serve them the same thing every day, but I tell them it's different. Monday it's chicken, Tuesday I tell them it's veal, Wednesday it's turkey. They can't remember anyway!

jokes.text: You're a slime, Louie.
jokes.setup: Louie has just described exploiting elderly nursing home residents
jokes.punchline: Alex's understated moral judgment
jokes.explanation: Appropriate moral response that's understated given Louie's horrible suggestion

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: That's a great way to build character, Louie.

jokes.text: My God, I know her! She's dyed her hair, but that's her.
jokes.setup: A nude painting is being auctioned
jokes.punchline: Jim loudly claims to recognize the model, causing embarrassment
jokes.explanation: Jim's inappropriate outburst at a refined auction creates perfect cringe comedy

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I know her! I know that woman!

dialogue.speaker: Auctioneer
dialogue.line: You do?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah, she owes me money!

jokes.text: Yike! Whew! How did that get in here? Yuck! Ugh!
jokes.setup: Their painting is presented for auction
jokes.punchline: The group loudly expresses disgust at their own investment
jokes.explanation: Perfect self-sabotage - they're trying to drive down the price of their own investment

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Look at that! It's hideous!

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Absolutely terrible. What were we thinking?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: This is the worst painting I've ever seen in my life!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: We paid good money for this garbage!

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I can't believe we thought this was art.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Nobody in their right mind would buy this thing!

jokes.text: I got one for it. How about 'Garbage'?
jokes.setup: The auctioneer mentions the painting is untitled
jokes.punchline: Louie loudly suggests 'Garbage' as a title
jokes.explanation: Louie's crude aesthetic judgment delivered at the worst possible moment

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Garbage!

jokes.text: One hundred bucks, and that's my final offer!
jokes.setup: The bidding has reached $1,500 and they're getting outbid
jokes.punchline: Louie dramatically lowballs with $100 as if he's negotiating at a car lot
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd misunderstanding of how auctions work - treating it like haggling at a used car lot

jokes.text: You're getting on my nerves!
jokes.setup: The auctioneer has just reprimanded Louie
jokes.punchline: Louie responds as if the auctioneer is the problem
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie entitlement - blaming the auctioneer for his own disruptive behavior

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Me? I'm not the problem here. You're the problem. You're up there with your little hammer, thinking you're so important, banging away like you own the place. Well, let me tell you something, pal — I've seen a lot of auctioneers in my day, and you're by far the loudest one.

jokes.text: You better hope you don't spend your twilight years at Sunset Acres!
jokes.setup: Someone outbids them at the last second
jokes.punchline: Louie threatens them with his nursing home
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback combining his business interests with a threat - darkly funny

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You think you're smart, huh? You outbid me? I got news for you, pal. You keep this up, you're gonna end up in a nursing home. And I know people. I'll make sure they serve you prune juice for every meal.

jokes.text: Look at your nice suit...! You probably got a maid and a butler! You got maids, you got everything!
jokes.setup: Louie is frustrated by losing the auction
jokes.punchline: His increasingly desperate class-based resentment spiraling into incoherence
jokes.explanation: Louie's class resentment boiling over into desperate, repetitive accusations

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I lost the auction! Me! Louie De Palma! To some Park Avenue penthouse dweller with more money than sense!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: These rich people, they don't know the value of a dollar. They just throw money around like it grows on trees!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I worked hard for every nickel I got. Blood, sweat, and tears! And some trust fund baby waltz in and outbids me like it's nothing!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: They got their fancy schools, their fancy clothes, their fancy everything. What do I got? This! A dispatcher's booth and a bad ulcer!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: The system's rigged, I tell ya. Rigged against the working man. Against guys like me who came up from nothing!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: And don't even get me started on their manners. Their etiquette. Their... their stupid monogrammed handkerchiefs!

jokes.text: Oh, no! That's not fair! That's not... God, why, God?
jokes.setup: The artist has just died, validating their investment theory
jokes.punchline: Tony's inappropriate outrage at missing out on profiting from death
jokes.explanation: Tony's inappropriate grief over missing a financial opportunity rather than the artist's death

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Wait a minute! He died? Why didn't anybody tell me? I could've made a fortune!

jokes.text: But, you know, if you guys were at Normandy, we'd all be eating strudel right now.
jokes.setup: Louie is lamenting being associated with losers
jokes.punchline: He suggests they would have lost WWII if they'd been involved
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation of Louie's loser theory to historical proportions - darkly funny and quotable

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know what my problem is? I'm surrounded by losers. If these guys had been in World War II, we would've lost to the Germans.

jokes.text: Hey, there, cookie. I want one of your classiest things here... Something by... Vincent Van Patten, or one of those guys.
jokes.setup: Louie wants to buy classy art to prove he's not a slob
jokes.punchline: He mispronounces Van Gogh as 'Vincent Van Patten' (a TV actor)
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie malapropism - confusing the famous painter with a B-list TV actor while trying to seem cultured

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'm gonna buy some classy art. Show people I'm not a slob.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'll get one of those Vincent Van Patten paintings.

tmdb_overview: On Elaine's advice, the cabbies pool their money to buy a painting.
insight: Season 2's densest episode: 45 jokes in 45 minutes anchor character chaos.
